title: Chemical Reactions for Aldehydes and Ketones

concepts.title: By Addition of NaHSO4
concepts.conceptBody:
Reactions

Addition Reactions

Addition of $\mathbf{NaHS}{{\mathbf{O}}_{\mathbf{3}}}$ 

Mechanism: 

 

  • Here $\text{SO}_{3}^{2-}$ is a stronger base than $\text{SO}_{3}^{2-}$ because of more electron density. Hence, $\text{SO}_{3}^{2-}$ will be the attacking species

Note: Hindered ketone does not give the reaction.

concepts.title: By Addition of HCN
concepts.conceptBody:
Addition of HCN 

 

Mechanism: 

$\text{HCN}\rightleftharpoons {{\text{H}}^{+}}+\text{C}{{\text{N}}^{-}}$

Note: 

(1) As done in the case of $\text{NaHS}{{\text{O}}_{3}}$, here also hindered ketone does not give the reaction.

Illustration:

concepts.title: By Addition of Grignard's Reagent
concepts.conceptBody:
Addition of Grignard's reagent RMgX 

 

  • Aldehydes form 2 alcohol (except $\text{HCHO}$ which gives $\text{RC}{{\text{H}}_{2}}\text{OH}$ ) (1 alcohol) while ketones form 3 alcohol.

concepts.title: By Addition of Alcohols
concepts.conceptBody:
Addition of Alcohols 

 

  • Hemiacetal is very reactive and easily forms acetal
  • The above reaction is not so favourable with ketones but can be favourable if 1,2 or 1,3 diols are used. With diols, cyclic ketals are formed.

 

Note: ketals are formed only by unhindered ketones. 

  • Acetals or ketals are stable in neutral or basic conditions but in acidic medium, they undergo acid catalysed cleavage similar to that of ethers to regenerate carbonyl compounds. Thus this reaction can be used to protect carbonyl groups.

For example

concepts.title: By Addition of Water
concepts.conceptBody:
Addition of water 

 

The rate of hydration decreases due to the presence of electron donating substituents while electron withdrawing substituents increase the rate.

Some examples of stable hydrated 

This is stable due to the intramolecular H-bonding.

The diving force of this reaction is the relief in angle strain on going from carbonyl compound ($C-C-C$ bond angle $={{60}^{{}^\circ }}$, compared with normal $\text{s}{{\text{p}}^{2}}$ bond angle of 120 ) to hydrate $\left( \text{C}-\text{C}-\text{C} \right.$ bond angle $=60$, compared with normal $\text{s}{{\text{p}}^{3}}$ bond angle of 109.5 .)

concepts.title: By Addition of Ammonia Derivatives
concepts.conceptBody:
Addition of ammonia derivatives 

 

Mechanism: 

 

  • \[\text{pH}\]should be controlled carefully. Medium should be neither too acidic nor too basic.
  • $\text{HCHO}$ reacts with $\text{N}{{\text{H}}_{3}}$ differently forming UROTROPINE (hexamethylene tetraamine)

  • The reaction of ammonia with aldehydes and ketones forms imines, but the products are unstable and cannot usually be isolated. If a secondary amine is used, an enamine is formed. An enamine has an amino group bonded to one of the carbons of a carbon-carbon double bond and can be said to form when enolic form of a carbonyl compound reacts.

The table give below uses the following abbreviations $(\text{A},\text{B},\text{C})$

concepts.title: Reaction of –NH2 with Carbonyl Compound
concepts.conceptBody:
Table : Reactions of $\mathbf{N}{{\mathbf{H}}_{\mathbf{2}}}\mathbf{G}$with carbonyl compounds

In aqueous acidic solution, an enamine is hydrolysed back to the carbonyl compound and secondary amine.

concepts.title: Reaction with PCl5 or SOCl2 or Pyridine
concepts.conceptBody:
Reaction with $\mathbf{PC}{{\mathbf{l}}_{\mathbf{5}}}\mathbf{ or SOC}{{\mathbf{l}}_{\mathbf{2}}}$/pyridine 

 

  • The products are geminal dihalides.

concepts.title: Reaction with SeO2
concepts.conceptBody:
Reaction with $\mathbf{Se}{{\mathbf{O}}_{\mathbf{2}}}$

$\text{C}{{\text{H}}_{3}}\text{CHO}+\text{Se}{{\text{O}}_{2}}\xrightarrow[\text{acid }]{\text{Acetic}}\text{OHC}-\text{Se}+{{\text{H}}_{2}}\text{O}$

The reaction is only given by methylene group adjacent to the carbonyl group.

concepts.title: Witting Reaction
concepts.conceptBody:
Witting Reaction 

It is a reaction used for converting carbonyl compounds to alkenes

concepts.title: Oxidation of Ketones
concepts.conceptBody:
Oxidation of Ketones 

In general, oxidation of ketones give a mixture of carboxylic acid.

  • During oxidative cleavage of mixed ketones, the lower alkyl group is retained by carbonyl group. This is known as Popoff’s Rule.

Oxidation with sodium hypohalite 

  • The reaction is given by aldehydes or ketones having group.
  • Hypohalite is a selective oxidizing agent as it does not attack carbon carbon double bonds.
  • This is also called haloform reaction

Baeyer - Villiger Oxidation 

ketones are oxidised by caro’s acid $\left( {{\text{H}}_{2}}\text{S}{{\text{O}}_{5}} \right)$ or peroxy benzoic acid $\left( {{\text{C}}_{6}}{{\text{H}}_{5}}\text{C}{{\text{O}}_{3}}\text{H} \right)$ to esters.

  • The insertion of oxygen atom takes place between the carbonyl group and the group having greater migrating tendency.

concepts.title: Formation of Dichloro Diphenyl Trichloroethane
concepts.conceptBody:
Formation of DDT

Chloral (trichloro acetaldehyde) on reaction with chlorobenzene in presence of${{\text{H}}_{2}}\text{S}{{\text{O}}_{4}}$gives dichloro diphenyl trichloroethane (DDT). The product is used as an insecticide for killing insects and mosquito.

It is used in household under the name DDT

concepts.title: Reduction of Aldehydes and Ketones
concepts.conceptBody:
Reduction

(a) Mild Reduction

  • This is carried out by reducing agents like${{\text{H}}_{2}}/\text{Pt},{{\text{H}}_{2}}/\text{Pd},\text{LiAl}{{\text{H}}_{4}},\text{NaB}{{\text{H}}_{4}}$ etc. and converts $\text{CO}$ to $\text{CHOH}$

  • Aldehydes give 1 alcohol and ketones give 2 alcohols.
  • \[\text{NaB}{{\text{H}}_{4}}\] and $\text{LiAl}{{\text{H}}_{4}}$ reduce $\text{C}=\text{O}$ bond without reducing $\text{C}=\text{C}$ bonds in the compound.
  • However, $\text{LiAl}{{\text{H}}_{4}}$ reduced the carbon-carbon double bond, which is in conjugation with carbonyl group only when the $\beta $-carbon bears an aryl group.

(b) MPV reduction: This is Meervein - Ponndroff - Verley reduction and it involves reduction of $\text{C}=\text{O}$ group to $\text{CHOH}$ without affecting the other reducible group in the molecule. The reagent used are, aluminium isopropoxide and isopropyl alcohol.

 

(c) Strong reduction: 

 

  • Various reducing agents employed are$\text{Zn}-\text{Hg}/\text{HCl}$(Clemmensen’s reduction);$\text{N}{{\text{H}}_{2}}-\text{N}{{\text{H}}_{2}}$/ KOH (wolf - Kishner reduction); Na/alcohol; HI/red P.

(d) Reduction to pinacol

concepts.title: Cannizaro's Reaction
concepts.conceptBody:
Cannizaro’s Reaction

$\begin{matrix} \text{2 moles of aldehydes} \\ \text{containing no }\alpha \text{-hydrogen} \\ \end{matrix}\underset{(O{{H}^{-}})}{\mathop{\xrightarrow[medium]{\text{Conc}\text{. basic}}}}\,\begin{matrix} \text{Undergo disproportionation to produce one} \\ \text{mole of alcohol and one mole of carboxylic acid} \\ \end{matrix}$

(1) $2\text{HCHO}+\text{NaOH}\xrightarrow{{}}\text{C}{{\text{H}}_{3}}\text{OH}+\text{HCOONa}$

(2) $2\text{PhCHO}+\text{NaOH}\xrightarrow{{}}{{\text{C}}_{6}}{{\text{H}}_{5}}\text{C}{{\text{H}}_{2}}\text{OH}+{{\text{C}}_{6}}{{\text{H}}_{5}}\text{COONa}$

Mechanism: 

Rocking Illustration:

1. $\text{CC}{{\text{l}}_{3}}\text{CHO}\xrightarrow{\overline{\text{O}}\text{H}}\text{ ? }$

{Here $\text{CCl}_{3}^{-}$is more stable than ${{\text{H}}^{-}}$, hence ${{\text{H}}^{-}}$is not removed, thus cannizaro reaction does not occur}

2. $\text{CH}\equiv \text{C}-\text{CHO}\xrightarrow{{{\ominus }_{\text{OH}}}}\text{? }$

{in this case also, is more stable than }

Hence, products are $\text{HC}\equiv \text{CH}$ and $\text{HCO}{{\text{O}}^{\ominus }}$

concepts.title: Cross Cannizaro's Reaction
concepts.conceptBody:
Cross Cannizaro’s Reaction

$\text{HCHO}+\text{PhCHO}+\underset{(\text{ conc}\text{. })}{\mathop{\text{NaOH}}}\,\xrightarrow{{}}\text{PhC}{{\text{H}}_{2}}\text{OH}+\text{HCOONa}$

This reaction yields benzyl alcohol and not benzoic acid because carbonyl carbon of formaldehyde is more electrophilic than that of benzaldehyde. 

So, $^{-}\text{OH}$ finally attacks at formaldehyde (steric affects can also be said to have a major role in this attack).

concepts.title: Intermolecular Cannizaro's Reaction
concepts.conceptBody:
Intramolecular Cannizzaro

Glyoxal on reaction with conc. $\text{NaOH}$ gives 2-hydroxy ethanoate by intramolecular cannizaro reaction. The product is a $\alpha $-hydroxy acid.

Rocking Illustration

$\text{C}{{\text{H}}_{2}}=\text{CH}-\text{CH}=\text{O}\xrightarrow[^{-}\text{OH}]{\text{conc}\text{.}}\text{? }$

Solution:

concepts.title: Aldol Condensation
concepts.conceptBody:
Condensation Reactions:

(1) Aldol Condensation: This is shown by aldehyde/ketones having atleast one $\alpha -\text{H}$ atom. Here, $\alpha -\mathbf{C}$ of one of the molecules joins with the carbonyl carbon of the other.

  • The aldol reaction is more favourable for aldehydes than for ketones because of more acidic $\alpha $-Hydrogen atoms and more electrophilic carbon.
  • The dehydration product is $\alpha ,\beta $ - unsaturated carbonyl compound due to the conjugated structure formed.

Mechanism:

concepts.title: Cross Aldol Condensation
concepts.conceptBody:
Cross Aldol

When two different carbonyl compounds are used, it is called cross-aldol condensation.

All the four products are $\beta $-hydroxy carbonyl compounds.

When one of the carbonyl compound does not have any $\alpha $-hydrogen, it cannot form carbanion and number of possible products reduces to two.

In the case of $\text{C}{{\text{H}}_{3}}\text{CHO}+\text{C}{{\text{H}}_{3}}\text{C}{{\text{H}}_{2}}\text{CHO}$, since $\overset{\ominus }{\mathop{\text{C}{{\text{H}}_{2}}}}\,\text{CHO}$ is more stable than $\text{C}{{\text{H}}_{3}}\overset{\ominus }{\mathop{\text{CHCHO}}}\,$, hence major product will be of $\overset{\ominus }{\mathop{\text{C}{{\text{H}}_{2}}}}\,\text{CHO}$

similarly,

But if is in excess then,

another way to obtain a single aldol product is to convert one carbonyl compound completely into carbanion using a strong base like LDA (Lithium diisopropyl amide). Thus, this carbanion attacks the other carbonyl compound.

because here all the substrate is converted into carbanions.

concepts.title: Intermolecular Aldol Condensation
concepts.conceptBody:
Intramolecular aldol condensation: When a compound has two carbonyl groups, it can undergo intramolecular aldol condensation in presence of dilute base (if$\alpha -\text{H}$atoms are present in the compound). An intramolecular reaction is readily favoured if the reaction leads to the formation of a 5 or 6 -membered ring.

 

{Hence, in intra-molecular aldolization, the major product is decided according to the stability of ring formed}

concepts.title: Claisen Condensation
concepts.conceptBody:
Claisen Condensation

2 moles of ester containing atleast $2\alpha $-hydrogen$\xrightarrow{{{\text{C}}_{2}}{{\text{H}}_{5}}\text{ONa}}\,\,\beta $-keto ester

Mechanism: 

Although it is expected to be$\beta $-ketoester as formed above, but the final product is the sodium salt of$\beta $-ketoester. This is because acetoacetic ester (having$\alpha $-hydrogen between two carbonyl groups) is much stronger acid than ethyl alcohol. So, the ester reacts with ethoxide ion to form ethyl alcohol and the anion of sodium ester. The salt is stabilized by resonance.

concepts.title: Mixed Claisen Condensation
concepts.conceptBody:
Mixed Claisen Condensation:

Just like mixed aldol, a mixed claisen condensation also takes place when two different ester are present. Here also, only one product will be formed when one of the ester has no $\alpha $-hydrogen and is taken in excess while the other ester is added slowly to the reaction mixture.

concepts.title: Dieckmann Condensation
concepts.conceptBody:
Dieckmann Condensation:

Intramolecular Claisen condensation of esters with $\alpha $-hydrogen atoms in the presence of sodium ethoxide leading to cyclization is called Dieckmann Candensation.

concepts.title: Perkin Condensation
concepts.conceptBody:
Perkin Condensation:

concepts.title: Tischenko Reaction
concepts.conceptBody:
Tischenko Reaction:

This reaction is only given by aldehydes, where 2 molecules of aldehydes undergo condensation type reaction to give an ester when treated with aluminium ethoxide or iso propoxide.

concepts.title: Halogenation Reaction
concepts.conceptBody:
Halogenation

(a) $\text{C}{{\text{H}}_{3}}\text{CHO}+3\text{C}{{\text{l}}_{2}}\xrightarrow{{}}\underset{Chloral}{\mathop{\text{CC}{{\text{l}}_{3}}\text{CHO}}}\,+3\text{HCl}$

(b) ketones can be halogenated in presence of dilute solution of bases or an acid catalyst.

$\text{C}{{\text{H}}_{3}}\text{COC}{{\text{H}}_{3}}+\text{Br}\xrightarrow[\text{acid}]{\text{base or}}\text{C}{{\text{H}}_{3}}\text{COC}{{\text{H}}_{2}}\text{Br}+\text{HBr}$

Mechanism: 

(1) In presence of base

(2) In presence of acid

Note: The above substitution can also be carried out using $\text{S}{{\text{O}}_{2}}\text{C}{{\text{l}}_{2}}$

concepts.title: Reformatsky Reaction
concepts.conceptBody:
Reformatsky Reaction

It is an addition reaction in which an organozinc reagent is used, instead of Grignard reagent, to attack the carbonyl group of an aldehyde or ketone. Because the organozinc reagent is less reactive than a Grignard reagent, a second nucleophilic addition to the ester group does not occur. The organozinc reagent is prepared by treating an $\alpha $-bromoester with zinc.

concepts.title: Beckmann Reaction
concepts.conceptBody:
Beckmann Rearrangement

An acid-induced rearrangement of oximes to give amides. When oximes are treated with acidic catalyst like${{\text{H}}^{+}},\text{PC}{{\text{l}}_{5}},\text{SOC}{{\text{l}}_{2}},\text{S}{{\text{O}}_{3}},{{\text{P}}_{2}}{{\text{O}}_{5}}$ etc., they are transformed into substituted amides. This reaction is related to the Hofmann and Schmidt Reactions and the Curtius Rearrangement, in that an electropositive nitrogen is formed that initiates an alkyl migration.

Example 

Mechanism: 

 

Oximes generally have a high barrier to inversion, and accordingly this reaction is envisioned to proceed by protonation of the oxime hydroxyl, followed by migration of the alkyl substituent “trans” to nitrogen. The $\text{N}-\text{O}$ bond is simultaneously cleaved with the expulsion of water, so that formation of a free nitrene is avoided.

The migrating group is always anti to $-\text{OH}\text{. }$
